title: Divergent War Aims
subtitle: The U.S., Israel, and the Strategic Logic of Divergence in the Iran Conflict
title_normalized: divergent war aims: the u.s., israel, and the strategic logic of divergence in the iran conflict

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

Short form of the policy brief title

description: This policy brief analyzes strategic divergence between the United States and Israel in the Iran conflict. It argues that the two actors remain operationally aligned but differ in war aims, time horizons, escalation thresholds, and definitions of success, while Iran pursues cost imposition to exploit coalition asymmetry over time.
abstract: The United States and Israel are often treated as strategically aligned in confronting Iran, but their war aims diverge in structure, time horizon, and acceptable levels of escalation. The United States prioritizes control sustainability: applying sustained pressure while maintaining escalation control, preserving coalition cohesion, and avoiding strategic overextension. Israel prioritizes deterrence restoration through coercive credibility, even at higher escalation risk. Iran’s strategy is best understood as cost imposition under asymmetry, designed to exploit divergence within the opposing coalition by amplifying differences in tempo, cost tolerance, and strategic objectives. The resulting pattern is not alliance breakdown, but strategic asymmetry within the coalition, in which continued operational coordination masks increasingly divergent strategic logics.

series_information.series_name: EPINOVA Policy Brief Series on Strategic Competition, AI-Enabled Warfare, and Information Conflict
series_information.series_description: Policy brief series translating EPINOVA research on strategic competition, AI-enabled warfare, information conflict, deterrence dynamics, escalation management, and global security governance into concise policy-facing analysis.

methodology.framework_name: Divergent War Aims and Coalition-Asymmetry Assessment Framework
methodology.source_basis: Policy-facing synthesis based on observed U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict dynamics, comparative assessment of U.S. and Israeli war aims, cost-imposition logic, threshold and tempo divergence analysis, and strategic interaction mapping.
methodology.case_context: U.S.–Israel–Iran conflict, U.S.–Israel operational cooperation, Iran’s cost-imposition strategy, coalition management, and escalation-control challenges.
methodology.core_problem: Operational alignment between allies can mask deeper strategic divergence when actors define success, acceptable costs, escalation thresholds, and time horizons differently under sustained conflict pressure.
central_claims: The United States and Israel are operationally aligned but strategically divergent in the Iran conflict.
U.S. strategy prioritizes control sustainability, escalation management, coalition cohesion, and avoidance of strategic overextension.
Israeli strategy prioritizes coercive credibility and deterrence restoration through visible and consequential pressure.
Iran pursues cost imposition under asymmetry to exploit coalition divergence over time.
The most likely near-term trajectory is continued operational cooperation alongside widening differences in tempo, thresholds, and risk tolerance.

analytical_lenses.name: Operational Alignment vs. Strategic Divergence
analytical_lenses.description: Distinguishes tactical and operational cooperation from underlying differences in war aims, end states, risk tolerance, and definitions of success.

analytical_lenses.name: Control Sustainability
analytical_lenses.description: Interprets the U.S. approach as an effort to maintain pressure while preserving escalation control, alliance cohesion, and strategic capacity across multiple theaters.

analytical_lenses.name: Coercive Credibility
analytical_lenses.description: Interprets the Israeli approach as a requirement to restore deterrence through visible and durable punishment under immediate threat conditions.

analytical_lenses.name: Cost-Imposition and Wedge Strategy
analytical_lenses.description: Analyzes Iran’s use of low-cost pressure, tempo management, perception effects, and threshold calibration to amplify coalition-management stress.

analytical_lenses.name: Threshold–Tempo–Asymmetry Interaction
analytical_lenses.description: Maps how differences in escalation thresholds and operational tempo generate strategic asymmetry within a formally cooperating coalition.

dimensions.name: Control Sustainability
dimensions.description: Assesses the capacity to apply pressure while maintaining escalation control, coalition cohesion, and avoidance of strategic overextension.

dimensions.name: Coercive Credibility
dimensions.description: Evaluates the perceived inevitability, visibility, and consequence of punishment used to restore deterrence.

dimensions.name: Threshold Divergence
dimensions.description: Captures differences among coalition actors regarding acceptable cost, sufficient outcomes, and tolerable escalation levels.

dimensions.name: Tempo Divergence
dimensions.description: Measures divergence in preferred operational pace, duration, and intensity between allied actors.

dimensions.name: Coalition-Management Stress
dimensions.description: Assesses the degree to which operational coordination masks growing strain in strategic synchronization and end-state alignment.

dimensions.name: Cost-Imposition Pressure
dimensions.description: Evaluates how sustained low-cost pressure forces adversaries to absorb cumulative fiscal, operational, and political burdens.

key_findings: The United States and Israel are operationally aligned but strategically divergent.
U.S. strategy prioritizes escalation control and control sustainability, while Israel prioritizes deterrence restoration through coercive credibility.
The divergence reflects structural constraints and threat environments rather than temporary policy disagreement.
Iran is pursuing a cost-imposition strategy designed to exploit coalition asymmetry and amplify divergence over time.
The likely near-term trajectory is continued operational cooperation alongside widening differences in tempo, thresholds, and risk tolerance.
The U.S.–Israel gap is not primarily about whether to act, but about where to stop.

Iran can gain strategic advantage without defeating the United States or Israel outright if coalition warfare becomes progressively more costly, politically difficult, and strategically less coherent.
The emerging divide is definitional, reflecting different understandings of what the war is, what it requires, and what it means to lose.
policy_recommendations: Recognize that alliance unity does not eliminate strategic divergence in war aims, end states, and escalation thresholds.
Treat escalation control as a coalition-management problem as well as a military problem.
Define more clearly which outcomes are sufficient for deterrence, sustainable for regional stability, and compatible with broader U.S. strategic commitments.
Incorporate Iran’s incentive to widen alliance asymmetry into coalition planning rather than assuming that increased military pressure automatically strengthens cohesion.
Align coalition strategy around shared understandings of acceptable escalation, tolerable costs, and conditions for termination.
Avoid excessive reliance on escalation that may widen strategic divergence and constrain long-term coalition effectiveness.

figures.title: U.S.–Israel–Iran Strategic Interaction Triangle
figures.description: Triangular conceptual diagram showing the distinct but interdependent strategic logics of the United States, Israel, and Iran: U.S. control sustainability, Israeli coercive credibility, and Iranian cost imposition. It highlights alliance coordination under divergent thresholds, direct coercion and deterrence contestation, and sustained cost pressure below full-scale war.
figures.source: Author’s conceptual reconstruction based on the threshold–tempo–asymmetry interaction framework
figures.note: Figure shown on page 5 of the PDF.

tables.title: Diverging Threshold Logics of War: United States vs. Israel
tables.description: Compares U.S. and Israeli strategic thresholds across time horizon, strategic objective, escalation preference, and primary constraint. The United States is characterized as long-term and system-oriented, focused on containment and stability, while Israel is characterized as short-term and threat-driven, focused on altering the security environment and restoring deterrence.
tables.note: Table shown on page 4 of the PDF.
